content:
{
  "title": "Dynamic Diagram-First Prompting for Enhanced Mathematical Reasoning in LLMs",
  "problem": "Current LLMs often falter on multi-step mathematical problems that benefit from visual or diagrammatic reasoning, as standard text prompts fail to guide models to generate and leverage intermediate visual representations.",
  "motivation": "Many complex mathematical problems, especially in geometry, combinatorics, and algebra, naturally lend themselves to diagrammatic thinking. However, current prompting methods rarely encourage LLMs to externalize or refer to such intermediate representations. Improving this ability could bridge the gap between human-like mathematical intuition and model outputs.",
  "method": "We propose a two-stage prompting method where the initial prompt instructs the LLM to generate a concise textual description of a relevant diagram or visual structure before any symbolic or numeric computations. A secondary, automatically constructed prompt then provides this diagram description as context, instructing the model to perform step-by-step reasoning conditioned on it. Optionally, text-to-image models can render the described diagram, and its caption can be fed back as an additional prompt input, simulating visual grounding. This dynamic diagram-first prompting guides the model to plan and reason visually before tackling the core mathematical task.",
  "experiment_plan": "We evaluate on subsets of GSM8K, MathQA, and geometry-heavy datasets (e.g., GeoBench), comparing against CoT, scratchpad, and diagram-free prompting. Metrics include answer accuracy, intermediate reasoning quality (via rubric-based scoring), and ablation on the use of generated diagrams. Human annotation will assess usefulness and correctness of generated diagram descriptions."
}
